title: Scaling Systems

content.question: What process will break at 10x, and what do you design now to prevent it?
sop: List your five core operating processes: fulfillment, onboarding, support, hiring, invoicing.
For each, ask whether effort rises 1:1 with volume, or stays flat as volume grows.
Flag any process where your personal time is the constraint, that's a system, not a scale-ready process.
Rebuild the flagged process as a template, tool, or delegated workflow before volume forces it.
Test on paper at 2x current volume, note exactly where it strains.
questions: What process will break at 10x, and what do you design now to prevent it?
Which process today requires you personally to touch every unit, and what happens to it at 10x volume?
If your customer count doubled overnight, which system would visibly snap first?
content.equation_example: Output (customers served, count) divided by Effort (your hours spent, count) equals Scalability ratio. Output 100 customers divided by Effort 40 hours equals 2.5. Higher/rising means each hour of your effort supports more output as you grow; lower/falling means growth demands proportionally more of your time, not less.
content.closing_statement: Build so growth outpaces your own effort.

caption:
Principle Nº 168: Scaling Systems

Systems must scale or break.

Ask yourself: What process will break at 10x, and what do you design now to prevent it?
